title: Chapter 31
text: Chapter Twenty- Eight R onin sat motionless in the worn leather chair, fingers digging into its arms. His processors blazed despite his outward stillness. Hours had passed with excruciating slowness. Rodriguez’s questions were brief, to-the-point, and seemingly endless. Throughout, Ronin’s thought-chains returned to Lara . There was a knock at the door . “Enter,” Rodriguez called . A woman dressed in a white coat came in. She approached the Colonel and whispered in his ear . Rodriguez’s gaze fell on Ronin. “Lara is in stable condition .” The woman stepped away and walked toward the door . “Then I’ll go see her now,” Ronin replied, rising . “We’re not finished here .” “I’m finished. I’ve answered all your questions — many of them numerous times — and I’ll answer more later. But, right now, I am going to see Lara .” The woman stood in the doorway, eyes flicking between Ronin and Rodriguez . “You can trust him to cooperate,” Newton said. “Allow him this .” At length, Rodriguez nodded, waving a hand at the woman. “ Take him .” Ronin followed her into the corridor. The tail of her lab coat drifted behind her as she wove through the hallways with surprising speed. There were fewer people than before in the large chamber, and different soldiers patrolled the catwalks. The woman led Ronin into the passageway with green paint . After walking through another collection of labyrinthine corridors, they finally arrived at a doorway with Infirmary painted on the wall beside it. They entered a low-ceilinged room that was divided up by curtains hanging from metal rails — Ronin counted forty such partitions, only six of them closed . Narrow, neatly-made beds stood in the open sections, surround by various pieces of equipment similar to what he’d seen in the unused room at the Clinic. In the far corner was machinery for bot repairs — more advanced, at a glance, than anything in Cheyenne . The woman led him to one of the closed curtains. A soft, steady beeping came from within. She stopped at the opening and turned to Ronin . “You need to understand that she’s still in serious condition. She’s stable, but that can change at any time. She’s got a broken arm, fractured ribs, several lacerations, and severe bruising. The swelling will go down, in time, and we don’t believe her vision will be affected .” Ronin nodded and moved to step past her, but she stopped him with a gentle hand on his chest . “She suffered trauma to her brain. She’s in a coma. We don’t know when — or if — she’ll wake .” Ronin’s processors halted, either unable or unwilling to assimilate that information. If he’d listened to Lara and left Cheyenne when she first spoke of it, if he hadn’t insisted on one more run, if he hadn’t allowed himself to grow so complacent that he missed the countless signs of danger all around … The woman sidestepped and opened the curtain. Ronin walked through the opening . Lara’s hair caught his attention first. It was clean and tied in a neat pony tail, standing out in bold contrast to the white bedding. Dark bruises marred the pale skin of her face. Both of her eyes were swollen shut, an inch-and-a-half long line of stitches marred her left cheek, and her left arm was splinted, wrapped against her chest in a sling . She was so still, apart from the shallow rise and fall of her chest and shoulders, looked so slight in the pristine bed, surrounded by medical equipment. A blanket covered her abdomen and legs, but he knew there was more bruising beneath it . He followed the tube in her right arm up to the jar of liquid suspended on a pole beside the bed, and then shifted his optics to the monitor displaying her heartrate. Its beat was slow, but steady . Gently, he placed his hand over hers. “Don’t leave yet .” She didn’t respond. Seconds dragged into minutes . “I’ll get you a chair,” the woman in the white coat said, drawing Ronin’s attention to the open curtain. Had she stood there the whole time ? “I don’t need to sit .” She lingered in the opening. “I’ll get one anyway, in case you change your mind. Otherwise, you might be standing for a long time.” Stepping back, she took hold of the curtain. “Talk to her. It’ll do her good to hear your voice .” The woman closed the curtain, and her soft footfalls faded as she left the room . Ronin squeezed Lara’s hand, reaching forward to brush a stray lock of hair from her face. He lightly ran a fingertip over the tiny bit of uninjured skin on her cheek . “Remember the ocean, Lara? I asked you what places you’d like to see, and that was your answer. You didn’t even have to think about it. I’ll take you there, once you’ve recovered. You can dance barefoot in the sand to the music of the waves. Collect as many seashells and bits of driftwood as you want. We can watch the sun set over the water, and pretend we’re the only two people in existence .” He struggled to picture it in his head, to piece together images from his memory to create the scene. How content would they be if they were the last two survivors ? The images eluded him, and the logical part of his mind listed the myriad of complications such a scenario would pose — how could he guarantee her food supply, or proper medical care? Would she be saddened if she never saw another flesh-and-blood person, if she never knew what it was like to carry a child, to see a family grow around her ? Another part of him set those things aside, if only for a fraction of an instant, and saw the happiness. The intimacy. The companionship . He loved Lara, and it didn’t matter if his emotions had begun as a simulation, didn’t matter if they were still classified as such. Whatever they were — whatever people like William Anderson called them — Ronin felt them all . “I spent a long time searching,” he said, rubbing his thumb over the back of Lara’s hand, “for my purpose. My programming. All of us were made for a reason.” He chuckled. “It’s not a reason I would have guessed…but it doesn’t matter anymore. I found what I was looking for before Newton told me any of that . “It was you , Lara. You’re my purpose, you’re my reason for carrying on, the reason I haven’t sat down in an abandoned building and never stood up again. I don’t think I’m selfish, normally, but I need you. I just found you, and I don’t think I can go any further without you .” Her only response was through the beeping of electronics, steady and indifferent . Twelve minutes and fifteen seconds later, the woman in the white coat returned with an ancient-looking chair. The cushion was flat and ragged, but the metal frame was solid and rust-free. She set it down quietly, several feet from the bed, and checked the medical equipment . Ronin sat in the chair to keep out of her way, fixing his optics on Lara’s unchanging face. It would take no effort to call up his memories of her dances, or the first time he’d heard her laugh, or any of the hundreds of other moments with her that he’d forever cherish, but he refrained. What would it bring, other than more pain? The past was gone, and could never be reclaimed, no matter how impeccable his recall . She couldn’t dance now , couldn’t smile, couldn’t even open her eyes or react to anything around her . “We’re doing everything we can,” the woman said. She’d been at the edge of his vision, watching him, for nearly a minute . “ I know .” “They told us it was rough on you getting here. There wasn’t much information at that point, but…from the first moment, our only priority was keeping her alive. That’s not going to change .” Ronin shifted his focus to the woman. His processors replayed her words twice, analyzing her tone, before understanding dawned on him. This was compassion. Sympathy. Traits that seemed sorely absent in the world, apart from in Lara herself . “What’s your name?” he asked . “ Cyndi .” “Thank you, Cyndi .” She smiled, and the sad gleam in her eyes reminded him of the blond synth who worked in the Clinic. Perhaps he’d been overly-harsh in his judgment of Mercy; her sympathy was likely just as genuine as Cyndi’s . “Thank Nancy. She’s the one who really saved this girl’s life.” She moved further into the small space and plucked something from a metal tray in the far corner. Ronin’s processors slowed at the flash of gold . Yours Until the End of Time . Cyndi held her hand out, the ring resting in the center of her palm. “We needed to remove it to bandage her ribs .” He stared at it, a painful reminder that he’d failed in his vow to protector her. Keeping his hand steady, he took the ring from Cyndi, and closed his fingers around it. “ Thank you .” “You’re welcome .” Cyndi left again, and Ronin relocated the chair to the beside. He took Lara’s hand again, seeking comfort in its warmth and finding little. He slipped the ring onto her finger and brought it to his lips . “Don’t leave yet,” he repeated . Four hours later, the curtain slid open again. Ronin turned his head slowly — reluctant to look away from Lara — to see Will, the younger of the two Andersons, peeking through the gap . “Ronin? Would you come with me, please ?” Turning back to Lara, Ronin shook his head. “I’m not ready to answer more questions yet .” The pulse monitor continued its gentle rhythm without variation . “I’m not here to interrogate you. My father and I noticed you were damaged when you came in. We’d like to get you repaired, while Lara sleeps .” Ronin glanced down at his torso. There was only a single breach on his chest, a hole large enough to fit his thumb into. The four on his back were smaller, but just as potentially problematic, and the skin there had been ripped apart to access his power cell. Somehow, nothing integral to his functioning had been damaged. The holes were simply invitations for dust and moisture to seep into his casing . His preferred response was refusal, paired with several potential excuses. He’d no inclination to leave her side. They wouldn’t let him sit here, indefinitely, until she awoke, but he intended to stay as long as he could. Eventually, they’d continue their interrogation. If enough time passed, logic suggested they’d demand some contribution from him to their continued survival; they’d ask a price for keeping her alive . Unfortunately, his contributions drastically increased the chances of getting more holes in his casing . “The equipment’s in the corner,” Will said, “maybe a hundred feet away. You’ll be close .” Ronin dropped a hand to his knee, clenching it into a fist. There was no logical reason to refuse. He’d seen the equipment when he entered the room. Lara’s monitors would be within earshot, and he could cross the distance in seconds, if the need arose . Leaning forward, he gently lifted her hand and brushed his lips over her knuckles. When he stood, he allowed his optics to linger on her for several more seconds before he finally turned to Will and followed him back to the repair machines . Will directed Ronin to climb onto the flat table in the center. He did so, laying facedown . “I know what it’s like,” Will said, examining the bullet holes . Ronin furrowed his brow . “ What ?” “The uncertainty. I know what it feels like. When my wife, Linda, went into labor with our daughter, there were complications. Doctor Cooper’s worked her share of miracles, but she’s realistic. She told me exactly what might happen, and what she planned to do to stop it. Having to wait through that…it was the worst time of my life. It was agonizing, feeling so helpless .” Helpless . It seemed an adequate word. For the first time in one hundred and eighty-five years, Ronin felt that, no matter what choice he made, no matter what actions he did or did not take, the outcome would be wholly unaffected. Lara would live or die, and he had no power over it . “This is normal?” Ronin asked finally . Will chuckled, turning away to power on a monitor and lift a small tool from a nearby tray. A camera on the end of the tool relayed Ronin’s internal damage to the screen . “Helplessness is part of the everyday human condition .” “I’m not human .” “Why? Because you’re made up of different parts ?” On the monitor, neat bundles of wiring and circuitry ran along segmented metal columns, all around the central box that housed Ronin’s CPU and data cores . “Those parts are just one thing on a long list of differences .” “Yes, in some ways. But an organic lifeform and a machine are not entirely different. Hell, if anyone knows, it’d be me.” Will guided the camera along the scrapes and grooves created by the bullets’ passage. “I’m the sixth generation of a family that’s devoted its entire life to robotics. The sixth William Anderson. You think you have identity issues?” He laughed again, it was a warm sound . The closest Ronin had heard was from Lara. Genuine laughter was a rare thing, it seemed, but it had a strange way of alleviating tension . “You’re not your father, or your grandfather, or any of them,” Ronin said . “Right. But I’m expected to be.” Will fell silent for a moment. “An inch off the trajectory of any of these, and you might’ve been in some serious trouble. These armor-piercing rounds they used everywhere during the war can chew through inner casings like they’re made of paper, at close range .” “Better me than her .” “I don’t think my great-great-great grandfather could’ve foreseen any of this, but I bet he would’ve been delighted over it .” “Over what?” Ronin asked. “Over what happened to the world ?” “No. He abhorred that. I mean the way bots have evolved. His work, when you get down to it, was the very basics of how you operate now. Everything in the time since…you’ve developed it on your own. You have a life . In the old world, just the thought of that was enough to cause widespread panic .” Will selected a few more tools, watching the monitor as he slipped them into the bullet hole and began the repairs. Sparks danced across Ronin’s electrodes, but only distantly . “Does that mean the war started because of bots ?” “I don’t think so. To be honest, it’s hard to say why it happened anymore, even with the records we have. I think it was building for a long time. Bots might’ve been what tipped it, but that was an excuse more than anything. The whole point is that you have free will. You can experience emotions, you have doubts and regrets, you can fall in love. That is what it means to be human. It was never about being an organic bipedal lifeform evolved from primates .” Ronin lay in silence, ignoring the vibrations pulsing through his insides as Will worked. It couldn’t be that simple, could it? He’d wandered for decades, only experiencing the merest hints of emotion, never knowing how strong they could be until Lara . Had he always possessed the capability for deeper feeling ? Perhaps the answer was simple — he’d always been capable, but it had taken Lara to give him something to care for. She’d sparked his emotions, and now they burned bright. She was the catalyst of his evolution . “You also have free will,” Ronin said. “You’re no more defined by your name than I am my parts .” “You’re right,” Will replied, smiling, “but I spent a lot of time worrying about it, anyway. It’s a lot to live up to. A family legacy that can easily eclipse anything I might do. What’s important, though, is that we’ve preserved his vision in this place. Bots and humans coexisting, having relationships, living side-by-side. I can’t deny that I’m proud to be a part of that .” “I owe much to you and your ancestors, Will Anderson .” Will laughed again. “Don’t get me wrong — I was relieved as hell when I found out we were having a girl. It broke the six-generation-chain of sons. Meant I didn’t have to make some excuse to explain to dad why I wasn’t going to name my kid William Joseph Anderson the Seventh .” Those generations had come and gone while Ronin walked the Dust. He’d been aware of the cycle of human life, but never considered it more than superficially. So many humans had joined together, built families and raised children, only for the process to repeat . “Newton told me there were humans and bots who came together to form families,” Ronin said . “Yeah. Nobody’s sure how many there were, before. But there’s about a dozen here on the base. One more, I guess, with you two here .” “Do they...adopt children?” Why was he concerned about it? Was this the result of raw emotion, or innocent curiosity ? “Not many orphans running around here, usually. There’s a way for male synths to reproduce, as long as there’s a donor. Not very complicated, really, and we’ve done it a few times over the years .” Ronin nodded, turning his head away from the monitor as Will continued the repairs. Would Lara be happy to learn that news? Would she embrace the idea, or reject it outright ? Did he accept it? Before Lara, love had been an airy, ethereal word with little meaning to him. Had he changed so much, already, that he was prepared to procreate and raise children? The thought of caring for human young was more unsettling than wandering the expansive wastes … Will and Ronin drifted between companionable silence and easy conversation as time passed. The automated machines took over after the internal repairs were completed, though Will remained at the controls to monitor their function. After his casing was sealed, Ronin entered the epidermal synthesizer — Will called it the reskinner , which seemed far more practical a name . As Ronin emerged from the reskinner, he glanced down at his torso. His skin was a single shade, now, result of his last visit to the Clinic. The realization came with a strange sense of loss . “Why did you perform the internal repairs by hand?” Ronin asked, pulling on his clothes. He’d been away from Lara for too long. The continued beeping of her heart monitor wasn’t enough to reassure him, but his curiosity was genuine . “Partly because it’s a skill that would be lost, otherwise. If we don’t know how to do it without machines to help, what’ll happen when these machines eventually break down? And for the same reason Doctor Cooper treats most of her patients by hand, I guess. It’s more…personal. If we don’t send our human patients to lie in a machine and receive care without ever seeing another person’s face, why should we do that to bots ?” While he walked back to Lara, Ronin analyzed his conversation with Will. Despite the mounting evidence, he had difficulty believing in the apparent truth of this place — here, bots and humans were considered equals. What-ifs would do him no good, but he couldn’t help wondering how things might have turned out if they’d known about this place sooner. How much trauma and pain could have been avoided ? He pulled open the curtain and stepped into the partition, raising his optics . A woman stood beside the bed, frowning down at Lara. She sighed . “There have been stories for a long time,” the woman said, turning to Ronin, “about this Warlord . Things he’s done. And, even though he was just a few miles away, it all seemed so unreal because we never saw it firsthand.” She ran a hand through her dark hair. The first signs of gray were visible at her temples. “This kind of brutality is unacceptable .” Ronin settled his gaze on Lara. “He did the same thing to her sister, but there was no one to help her in time .” The woman shook her head. “We’re not perfect here. Sometimes, fights break out. Every now and then a man hits his wife. Jack and his people get things back in order pretty quickly, but this …this is unlike anything I’ve ever seen .” She brushed her fingertips over the back of Lara’s hand, touch lingering for a second — enough for even Ronin to recognize the true compassion in the gesture. Drawing her hand away, she walked around the bed and held it out to Ronin . “I’m Nancy. Ronin, right ?” It took nearly a second to dredge up the data for an appropriate response. He took Nancy’s hand — as careful as ever to regulate the strength of his grip — and shook it. “Yes. You’re the Doctor Cooper everyone’s been talking about ?” “I am. The person with the drugs is always the most popular.” She smiled, but the expression wavered. “I’m sorry. I try to keep things light, but it’s inappropriate, given the situation .” “I understand. No apology necessary .” “We’re doing everything in our power to make her well again. Unfortunately, it’s just a waiting game , now .” “I know. Thank you, Nancy. Whatever happens.” Sorrow panged across his circuits at those last two words. Were they an admission of defeat? An acceptance of the possibility that she wouldn’t wake up, that their time together was done forever ? No. I won’t accept that . “She’s a fighter,” Nancy said. “I hear you are, too. That means a lot. Hell, if you hadn’t brought her here so fast, I don’t think she’d have survived the night .” They lapsed into silence, and Ronin watched Lara’s still form, a gentle ambience provided by the machinery . “I’ll give you some space,” Nancy said, after a minute. “You probably know already, but Jack’s not going to wait much longer to question you again. For now, I’m going to recommend that the best thing for Lara is to hear your voice and feel your touch. That’ll keep him at bay for a little while more .” She walked past him, pausing for a moment to place a hand on his shoulder and squeeze gently. Then she exited the partition, sliding the curtain closed behind her . It was jarring to face so much understanding, so much compassion, from so many people at once. He was a bot, but these people didn’t seem to make any distinction between metal and organic . He slid the chair over to the bed and sat, grasping Lara’s hand. None of that compassion would make a difference if she didn’t recover. Hers was the only touch he craved, the only understanding he needed .
